data.title: NerdWallet Is Becoming A Cash Flow Powerhouse
data.description:
In the rubble of fintech's post-2021 comedown, most investors still see NerdWallet (NRDS) as a glorified affiliate network - one that relies on SEO and content marketing to drive clicks and revenue.

In some respects, this is a fair assessment, as the company does generate high-quality financial content that helps people make better decisions in numerous financial categories, like credit cards, bank accounts, insurance, mortgages, and more.

In return, financial partners pay through the nose for leads and clicks that the NRDS generates.

It's a smart, simple business model.

However, in the last few quarters, NRDS has begun looking at itself more strategically. While the company's financial content engine remains robust, the firm doesn't capture nearly as much LTV as the partners to which it sells leads.

This is changing. In recent quarters, NRDS has verticalized the company, acquiring downstream loan, credit, and financial marketplaces that allow the firm to capture more value from the leads it generates.

At the same time, NRDS has also implemented a sticky app experience, a paid NerdWallet+ plan, and more algorithmically accurate recommendations for products and services to help users optimize their financial lives.

All of these moves have far-reaching implications for NRDS's long-term financials.

NRDS has done well up to this point in building out a high margin content business focused on SEO, but we expect that V2 of NerdWallet will begin to produce material shareholder returns as the company internalizes lead flow and accesses much better unit economics.

For its part, the stock has largely been 'range bound' since IPO in November 2021, despite the progress management has made in growing revenues:

Now, with considerably improved unit economics, incredibly high gross margins, and a strong growth / profitability lever that management can pull whenever they want, we think the company is positioned well to deliver strong shareholder returns going forward.

Today, we'll highlight NRDS's business model, outline the company's valuation - which we think appears attractive - and explain why this retail focused, 'financial guidance' platform looks like a 'Strong Buy'.

Sound good? Let’s dive in.

Financials

Let’s start by taking a deeper look at what NRDS does, because we’ve always been impressed with the core business model.

In short, NerdWallet produces retail friendly content about financial products across a number of verticals, including credit cards, bank accounts, insurance products, mortgages, investment accounts, and more.

The company writes content about these product verticals for people in the United States, the United Kingdom, Canada, and Australia.

If you’ve ever googled something like 'best options trading broker USA', or 'best credit card for cashback' you’ve likely seen NRDS content strategy at work.

A huge percentage of the company's traffic comes from organic SEO - that is, Google searches and Bing searches - and in the past, NRDS has been able to monetize this immense traffic by offering links and partnerships with financial product companies across the ecosystem.

For example, let’s say someone googles 'best travel credit card rewards 2025' and ends up on a NerdWallet article comparing top credit cards for travel. In that article, NerdWallet will have placed several links with calls to action, and for every fulfilled referral - that is - for every customer who clicks one of those links and then actually opens a credit card, NRDS receives a hefty payout for the lead.

It’s a business with incredible gross margins, given that the cost of serving this content to the wider financial web is relatively low, and singular investments in content can endure over time, generating strong recurring revenues.

Since IPO, NerdWallet has consistently earned gross profits of more than 90%, which is one of the highest gross margin businesses we’ve ever seen:

This, in turn, gives management a tremendous amount of flexibility when it comes to controlling growth versus profitability. In practice, with margins this high, management can choose how much of that gross profit should be re-invested vs. returned to shareholders. Historically, management has chosen to re-invest almost everything, hence the strong top line growth but breakeven operating profile:

While this is all well and good, re-investing for growth appears to have had diminishing returns.

In response to this, management has recently made a number of strategic moves, including buying smaller financial product marketplaces and integrating them into the company's traffic engine.

As a result, while its business has gotten slightly more complex, NRDS has seen an incredible lift in per-lead LTV, which has re-energized top line growth in recent quarters. You can see this from mid-2024 onwards on the blue TTM revenue chart above.

In fact, in Q1 of this year, Next Door Lending, one of NRDS's recent acquisitions, contributed significantly to the company's organic revenue growth, even in a period where mortgage rates were high:

Banking has continued to perform well, with products like high-yield savings accounts showing durable demand three years removed from a zero-interest rate environment. At the same time, this quarter we saw some green shoots in other areas of the business that have been challenged, such as personal loans, where we saw a return to growth after declining 51% year-over-year in Q4, '24 and mortgages, where our acquisition of Next Door Lending contributed significantly to our 23% year-over-year increase in revenue even as mortgage rates remained elevated.

Fundera and On The Barrelhead, two other marketplaces, have also shored up NRDS's finances in a period of relative cyclical weakness.

Not only that, but these vertical integrations do more than just boost longtail revenue, they also expand NRDS's overall market opportunity, which is key:

Finally, NRDS transformed from a content engine into a 'platform', complete with a mobile app. This provides more personalized financial guidance to people via integration with current accounts and credit information, which allows for more customized recommendations for financial products.

In combination with a premium offering that allows for users to earn higher interest rates at partner banks and institutions', NRDS has done a great job at improving its long-term recurring revenue opportunity.

Right now, analysts are anticipating 15%, 8%, and 4% YoY top line growth over the next three years, which we think appears incredibly low relative to the market opportunity and the new sales opportunities that have come about from the acquisitions:

At the same time, while NRDS already produces positive operating cash flow, we expect these moves to boost NerdWallet's bottom line profitability rapidly. Over the next three years, analysts are currently anticipating EPS to grow to $0.89 by December 2027, but we think this materially undercounts all of the recent strategic moves:

All in all, we think NerdWallet is primed to grow revenues in the high teens YoY over the next few years, while rapidly increasing earnings per share as a result of improving operating leverage and the more economical LTV/CAC ratios.

The future looks bright on an organic basis.

Valuation

Not only that, but we think that NRDS appears to be one of the cheaper companies on the market.

At a base level, NRDS earns roughly $70 million in free cash flow per year, and trades at a market cap of roughly $780 million:

Net out the nearly $100,000,000 in cash on the balance sheet, and you’re left with a single-digit free cash flow multiple for the stock.

From there, if you anticipate even modest increases in revenue and net income, we see the outcome being twofold. First off, in a vacuum, organic growth should lead to higher share prices as stronger business results are reflected linearly. This makes sense. We're anticipating mid-teens top line growth, which should reflect in the share price if the multiple stays stable.

However, with companies that are inflecting on GAAP profitability, we’ve also frequently seen rapid multiple expansion. If companies can convince the market that it's reaching sustained bottom line profitability, then investors will rerate shares higher.

Given NRDS's financial profile, we expect continued organic growth as the company executes its verticalized vision, which should include consistent GAAP bottom line income.

That way, NRDS could see combined growth in the numerator and denominator of the P/E multiple, which would likely drive serious appreciation for shareholders.

Nominally NRDS appears inexpensive for the current results investors are getting from management, but versus where the business could be in the coming years, we think shares are underpriced. This goes double when you consider NRDS's high gross margins, strong product market fit, and improving unit economics.

Overall, between the company's robust prospects and attractive multiple, we think shares look like a 'Strong Buy'.

Risks

Of course, there are risks associated with investing in a stock like NRDS. Here are a couple to be aware of.

While the company has done a good job at trying to in-house its current traffic flow and retain users by getting them to sign up into the company's app experience, NRDS is still downstream from any algorithm changes that Google might make to its search algorithm. Additionally, any AI changes - or changes in user search behavior towards AI - could also have an unknown impact on NerdWallet, given that over 70% of the firm's traffic comes from SEO.

This is an important space to watch, and no doubt the company knows this, but NRDS is - to some degree - at the mercy of larger tech titans.

Additionally, a considerable piece of the investment thesis here assumes both growing revenues and improvements in bottom line profitability. Obviously, NRDS has done a good job growing revenues historically, but it remains to be seen whether or not management will be able to produce significant bottom line profits over the short and medium term. The company's free cash flow is nice, but we’d like to see strong, continued GAAP profitability. Without that, shares may continue to languish.

Summary

Overall, between NerdWallet's incredibly high gross margins, improving unit economics, and overall growth opportunity, we're quite bullish on the firm's organic prospects.

Trading at a sub-10x EV/FCF ratio, we also believe the shares look quite cheap from a nominal perspective, which could also allow for considerable multiple expansion.

While there are some risks, we ultimately believe that most investors should have some NRDS in their portfolio.

Thus, our 'Strong Buy' rating.

Stay safe out there!

data.title: On Watch: Zinc Could Face Market Turbulence Akin To Copper
data.description:
US President Donald Trump said July 8 that the administration would imminently impose a 50% tariff on copper product imports, marking a pivotal moment for the global copper market. In response, copper prices on the COMEX surged more than 13% on the same day, to exceed $12,445/t, a new record. The premium over the London Metal Exchange (LME) cash price reached a record $2,520/t; it could potentially widen to $5,000/t, or a premium of around 50%. Futures contracts had been reflecting expectations of a possible 25% tariff, but the 50% announcement took the market by surprise. This spike in COMEX copper prices underscores the scale of investor anxiety over rapid changes in trade policy and their implications for supply chains and the future market balance.

The take

➤ Trump's tariff announcement drove COMEX-LME arbitrage above $2,500 per metric ton, a record high.

➤ COMEX-LME arbitrage could reach around $5,000/t, once US domestic stocks are depleted.

➤ Tariff expectations have led the US to stockpile in the year to date; refined copper imports are up by more than 500,000 metric tons year-over-year.

Following Trump's remarks, US Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick stated that copper tariffs are expected to be implemented by late July or Aug. 1. This policy, following a lengthy Section 232 investigation, is intended to reduce US reliance on foreign copper — particularly refined copper, which is vital for national security and infrastructure. The tariffs are expected to be applied broadly, covering refined copper cathodes, wire, rods and semi-finished copper goods, mirroring earlier actions on steel and aluminum. The Department of Defense has underscored copper’s strategic importance, highlighting it as the second most utilized material in its supply chain.

The announcement of the Section 232 investigation had already triggered dramatic shifts in global copper inventories and trade flows. In the first half of 2025, the US market responded by accelerating copper imports, leading to an increase of over 500,000 metric tons compared to the same period of 2024. This surge has pushed COMEX-registered inventories to over 200,000 metric tons, a seven-year high. Such stockpiling has resulted in a glut in the US while depleting inventories elsewhere.

Stocks at the LME have fallen sharply, down more than 60% in the year to date and standing at just above 100,000 metric tons on July 8. In China, the negative arbitrage between the Shanghai Futures Exchange (SHFE) and LME prices has discouraged imports. Chinese smelters have been exporting cathode to LME warehouses in Asia to capitalize on higher profits rather than selling domestically.

In the first five months of 2025, copper product imports into the US increased more than 65% year-over-year, with refined copper accounting for nearly 70% of the volume. Refined imports grew 130% year-over-year in the same period and 8.3% month over month in May. Chile is by far the largest supplier of refined copper to the US, The persistently high copper imports to the US are expected to decline following the imposition of tariffs. It remains unclear whether these countries will be exempted from 50% US tariffs. If not, they are likely to redirect their copper to other major consumers, such as the EU and China, aiding in replenishing LME and SHFE inventories. This redirection could pose a headwind for copper prices in these markets. As of July 9, the LME three-month and SHFE copper prices dropped 2.1% and 1.4%, respectively, compared to the previous day.

Looking ahead, the US decision to impose a 50% tariff on copper imports is causing fragmentation in the global copper market, creating sharp regional price dislocations and amplifying volatility. As the tariff regime takes hold, US copper inventories are expected to plateau and then gradually draw down as imports slow sharply and domestic supply struggles to keep pace with demand. Stocks in the rest of the world, particularly on the LME, are likely to gradually recover as trade flows adjust to the new tariff landscape. The premium of COMEX over LME is expected to persist, with the arbitrage spread potentially widening to reach 50% over the LME cash price, once the excess pre-tariff imported inventory is destocked.

A fall in US copper imports could encourage increased scrap usage and possibly restarts at US smelters, but these responses will take time and are unlikely to fully offset the loss of imports in the short term. Recent executive orders have set a clear direction to boost domestic copper supply. The administration has invoked emergency powers to streamline permitting and regulatory processes, notably expanding the FAST-41 program to accelerate federal reviews for major mining projects. This initiative now covers high-volume copper ventures such as the Resolution project in Arizona and the Pebble project in Alaska, both of which have faced years of permitting delays. By granting these projects FAST-41 status, the government aims to cut through bureaucratic hurdles and ensure timely decisions, potentially unlocking new domestic production that could eventually supply a significant share of US copper demand. It is unlikely that these operations could come online within the next four or five years, however.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Darden: Impressive Same-Restaurant Sales Performance In A Tough Economy (Upgrade)
data.description:
The S&P 500 is notching a new record almost daily, but amid broad market optimism, the macroeconomy remains on shaky ground: and nowhere has that been more apparent than in the restaurant industry, where consumers are pushing back against price increases and major chains like Chipotle (CMG) have reported weaker same-restaurant sales growth.

And yet, amid this backdrop, Darden Restaurants (DRI), the parent company behind Olive Garden and Longhorn Steakhouse, is doing quite well. Fresh off the acquisition of Chuy's Tex Mex, the company has reported improving same-restaurant sales growth amid margin accretion - two factors that set the company apart from struggling peers. Year to date, the stock is up ~10%:

I last wrote a bearish note on Darden in April, when the stock was trading at similar ~$200 levels. Since then, Darden has posted a very impressive rebound in same-restaurant sales: well above even the acceleration that the company itself was planning for. At the same time, Darden is also managing well through a period of rising input costs, particularly in beef: despite this, the company has issued a robust outlook for the current year FY26 (which is the year for Darden ending in May 2026). With this in mind, I'm upgrading Darden to a neutral rating.

To me, at current share prices, I see a more balanced bull and bear case for Darden. On the positive side for the company:

  • Darden is a diversified restaurant company with a portfolio of brands for a variety of tastes: Its restaurants encompass steakhouses, brewpubs, Italian, seafood, and now Tex-Mex. It welcomes diners at various price points, and its broad portfolio is national in scope.
  • Delivery opportunity: The company recently signed a broad delivery agreement with UberEats to boost its off-premise sales. Though I was initially skeptical of the incrementality of this deal, the recent surge in same-restaurant sales growth has proven that the proliferation of delivery services is truly improving Darden's everyday dining appeal.

At the same time, however, we are mindful of the following risks:

  • Inflation concerns: I'm particularly concerned about two core inputs: beef, which is Darden's largest food cost at ~25% of its COGS and is expected to inflate in the mid single digits in FY26; and labor - especially as inflation causes a vicious cycle for restaurant operators where minimum wages keep rising.
  • Competition: The restaurant space is incredibly crowded, and each of Darden's brands faces competition from both local independent restaurants as well as other chain conglomerates such as The Cheesecake Factory (CAKE).

From a valuation perspective, Darden is fair at best - which is ultimately what keeps me sidelined on the stock's prospects. As shown in the chart below, the company is guiding to $10.50-$10.70 in pro forma EPS for FY26, the midpoint of which represents 19% y/y growth against $8.88 in EPS in FY25 (though we note that FY25 only had a partial-year benefit from the Chuy's acquisition, so comps will get tougher once Chuy's is fully lapped in October 2025).

This outlook is predicated on 7-8% y/y total revenue growth, underneath which is 2.0-3.5% y/y same-restaurant sales growth. The company is planning on opening 60-65 new restaurants, or ~3% location growth against its current portfolio of ~2.2k restaurants.

Against the midpoint of its outlook, Darden trades at 19.1x FY26 P/E.

The stock is sitting a bit below a number of fast-casual/fast food peers like Chipotle and McDonald's (MCD), which have long sustained a premium to full-service dining competitors. However, Darden is trading at a slightly richer multiple than Cheesecake, which has similar low single digit same restaurant sales growth (and is also benefiting from a recent push to off-premises delivery orders).

All in all, I think Darden's improvement in same-restaurant sales growth and its resilience in margins amid a very inflationary environment gives the stock a decent chunk of downside protection. At the same time, however, I'd prefer to wait for Darden to come down to a mid-teens P/E multiple before I consider entering the stock.

Q4 Recap

Let's now go through Darden's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Darden's revenue grew 11% y/y to $3.27 billion, ahead of Wall Street's expectations of $3.26 billion (+10% y/y) by a one-point margin. Where we were duly impressed, however, was in the company's same-restaurant sales growth: which, as a reminder, measures revenue growth from restaurants in the existing portfolio, excluding the contribution from newly opened and newly closed stores.

Darden's overall portfolio of same-restaurant sales improved to 4.6% y/y growth in Q4. This not only beat the company's internal guidance of >3% SRS by a wide margin, but also accelerated nearly four points versus just 0.7% y/y growth in Q3. We note that the flagship Olive Garden brand accelerated more than six points to 6.9% y/y growth (from just 0.6% growth in Q3), while LongHorn Steakhouse also improved four points to 6.7% y/y growth, from 2.6% in Q3. The company did experience a drag in its "fine dining" portfolio (which consists of Capital Grille, Ruth's Chris Steakhouse, and Eddie V's Prime Seafood) which declined -3.3% y/y (from -0.8% y/y in Q3), though this is largely to be expected in a waning macroeconomy, and is only a small ~10% slice of overall sales.

Delivery has been critical to Olive Garden and Darden's recovery, combined with a thoughtful marketing push. Per CEO Ricardo Cardenas' remarks on the Q4 earnings call (key points highlighted):

The return of Olive Garden's buy one take one offer for the first time in 5 years, combined with the continued strength of off-premise, drove their impressive sales during the quarter. Olive Garden's marketing strategy to meaningfully communicate strong value, create urgency and introduce food news continue to resonate as guests are motivated by news and compelling price points [...] During buy one take one, Olive Garden same-restaurant sales gap versus the industry increased to 450 basis points.

With delivery available nationwide, the entire quarter, Olive Garden continued to see order volume grow week-to-week while retaining higher-than-average sales per transaction versus curbside pickup orders. This, combined with continued growth in catering and curbside to go, drove overall takeout sales to grow nearly 20% over last year.

At the end of the quarter, Olive Garden launched a campaign to promote delivery across multiple channels, including television advertising, with a compelling and memorable offer, 1 million free deliveries, partially funded by Uber. We continue to see strong incrementality with average weekly deliveries per restaurant nearly doubling during the last 2 weeks of the quarter."

Considering Darden's guidance for FY26 calls for only 2.0-3.5% same-restaurant sales growth (the midpoint of which is ~2 points below what Darden actually achieved in Q4), we think there may possibly be upside to Darden's forecast if current delivery-driven strength continues.

And as shown in the chart below, Darden also achieved a 50bps y/y increase in restaurant-level adjusted EBITDA margins. The company impressively achieved 70bps of leverage across F&B costs and labor costs, despite inflationary pressure.

Currently embedded into Darden's outlook for FY26 is mid-single digit inflation in beef and seafood costs (one-third of the company's core spending), and low single digit inflation in nearly every other category.

Key Takeaways

We're incredibly encouraged by Darden's improving performance, particularly in the Olive Garden segment, which is Darden's single largest brand at over ~40% of revenue. Delivery has clearly expanded Darden's addressable market and dining frequency, allowing the company to capitalize on economies of scale despite inflationary pressures in almost every category of Darden's spending.

Still, with Darden trading at ~19x P/E and slightly richer than Cheesecake, I'm wary to pull the trigger and buy Darden now. Keep monitoring this stock from the sidelines, and wait for the next major dip (which may come very suddenly in an overheated stock market) before diving in.

data.title: CVB Financial: FY Earnings Likely To Be Stable - Buy
data.description:
Earnings of CVB Financial (CVBF) will likely be stable this year. As I’ll discuss in greater detail below, I’m expecting the balance sheet growth to be low and the margin to be rangebound, which will keep earnings growth subdued. Overall, I’m expecting an EPS of $1.44 for 2025, almost unchanged from last year.

Outlook for Organic Loan Growth Remains Neutral to Negative

CVB Financial’s total loan balance declined in size for a sixth consecutive quarter in 2Q’25. The company has historically relied on mergers and acquisitions for growth, with organic loan growth being very low or negative. Over the last decade, in most of the years without acquisitions, the loan balance has actually declined in size.

As there have been no announcements of any upcoming transactions, I think M&A is out of the equation for loan growth in 2025. The management did mention in the conference call that they were looking for acquisition targets in California and outside of California; however, as nothing has been finalized so far, I don’t think they’ll be able to rush an acquisition and complete it this year. The management mentioned that it might make an M&A announcement by the end of this year. Therefore, I'm expecting an acquisition in the second half of next year, which seems like a reasonable time frame to me.

As for organic growth, the outlook is neutral to slightly negative. CVB Financial, through its subsidiary Citizens Business Bank, operates in Southern California, with a concentration in Los Angeles, Orange, and San Bernardino counties. Further, the company mostly focuses on Commercial Real Estate loans.

The unemployment rates in CVB Financial’s main markets have mostly been on a downtrend so far this year. Nevertheless, they’re currently not much better than in the same period last year.

The Southern Californian economy is reliant on service industries, especially Entertainment and Healthcare. Agriculture also plays an important role in CVB Financial’s markets. The services PMI index has mostly declined this year, and at 50.80, it is currently quite close to the contractionary territory, which is below 50.0.

Considering these factors, I’m expecting the loan balance to continue to decline this year.

Deposit growth has been low, but positive so far this year. Although I’m expecting the total loan balance to decline this year, I think deposit growth can continue to remain positive because of inflation. I think the tariffs will boost inflation, which will push up NOMINAL deposits. I think CVB Financial will have to park the funds raised through deposit growth in securities. The following table shows my balance sheet estimates.

Both Loan Yields and Deposit Costs Likely to Barely Move

CVB Financial’s net interest margin was stagnant at 3.31% in 2Q’25, unchanged from 1Q’25, as mentioned in the earnings release. I’m expecting the margin to continue to remain rangebound even as market interest rates decline. The futures market is currently mostly expecting 50 basis points of Fed funds rate cuts later this year, as shown by CME’s FedWatch tool.

One reason why I’m expecting the net interest margin to remain rangebound is that there isn’t enough space for deposit costs to decline. Non-interest-bearing deposits make up more than 60% of total deposits, which makes the deposit cost sticky. Historically, the deposit cost has not moved much as the Fed funds rate has changed, as shown in the chart below taken from the earnings presentation.

The second reason why I’m expecting the margin to be rangebound is that, like the deposit cost, the loan yield is also not likely to move much. Only 24% of Commercial Real Estate loans will mature or see a rate reset in the next 24 months, as mentioned in the earnings presentation.

Considering these factors, I’m expecting the net interest margin to remain unchanged in the second half of 2025 from the 2Q’25 level.

Expecting Earnings To Remain Stable

Last year’s earnings were mostly in line with the expectations given in my last report about the company, released in June 2024. This year, I’m expecting the earnings to barely change as the margin will likely remain rangebound and the balance sheet will grow only slightly on the back of deposits (and securities).

I’m expecting the provision expense to continue to remain near zero for the remainder of this year. Firstly, because I’m not expecting much loan origination. Secondly, despite the exposure to Los Angeles, the wildfires have barely had any material impact on the loan portfolio’s credit quality. The asset quality ratios continued to remain stable in the first half of 2025, as disclosed in the earnings release.

I’m also assuming that the non-interest income will remain rangebound near the last reported level, and the efficiency ratio will remain close to last year’s level of 46.5%.

Overall, I’m expecting CVB Financial to report an EPS of $1.44 for 2025, barely changed from last year.

The biggest risk to earnings is the ongoing trade policy uncertainty, which can keep businesses in a wait-and-see mode. Businesses may not want to make big decisions involving real estate loans in an uncertain environment. Consequently, there is a risk that the total loan balance can decline even more than expected.

Maintaining A Buy Rating

I last wrote a report on CVB Financial in June 2024, wherein I adopted a buy rating. As expected, the stock price rallied after my report’s issuance. In fact, the stock price has grown by 18% since my last report. Back in June of 2024, the operating environment was very different, as the US government at that time was quite different from the current administration. Therefore, my previous investment thesis is no longer valid. To update my investment thesis, I’m going to use the historical average price-to-earnings (“P/E”) and price-to-tangible book (“P/TB”) multiples given below.

Multiplying the average P/E multiple by my EPS forecast gives a target price of $18.3 for the end of 2025.

Multiplying the average P/TB multiple by my TBVPS forecast gives a target price of $23.6 for the end of 2025.

The average price target for the end of this year comes out to $20.9, which suggests an 8.6% price upside from the July 25 closing market price. CVB Financial also offers a good dividend yield of 4.1%. Taking the sum of the price upside and dividend yield gives a total expected return of 12.8%. Hence, I’m maintaining a buy rating on CVB Financial.

To conclude, the company’s earnings are likely to be stable this year, with little change seen in balance sheet size and the net interest margin. The market price is currently low enough to make the stock’s valuation attractive. Hence, a buy rating seems justified.

data.title: YBIT Seems The Least Appealing Bitcoin Option Strategy
data.description:
“But did you know that Bitcoin can become a yield‑generating asset?”

That was the opening remark from a colleague of mine, someone “purely” from the banking world, just back from a road show of his network. He then told me about YieldMax™ Bitcoin Option Income ETF (YBIT): first, it made me reflect on how here on Seeking Alpha we stay one step ahead when it comes to “financial engineering”; then, on how option income strategy ETFs are becoming an increasingly popular component, even in institutional circuits; and finally, on how little people actually understand what we’re talking about.

My opinion? Yes, the strategic approach proposed by YBIT is interesting, but it’s not suitable for every type of portfolio, and, above all, it’s a step behind other solutions: in my view, BTCI positions itself better. But before explaining why, you need to know that …

The Vision Of …

YieldMax is always very clear: the primary goal is to create a current monthly income. Tracking Bitcoin, we could say, is a sort of side question for the management.

And I have to say, the way they achieve this goal is more articulated than what has been done with other ETFs: simply because it’s “dynamic.” That is, they don’t use a single strategy; instead, they have a book of alternatives, and in this it reminds me a bit of LFGY, a YieldMax ETF that I honestly appreciate. According to the prospectus, YBIT can make use of synthetic covered calls: buying calls + selling puts to replicate the underlying ETP without physically owning it. But also the classic standard covered call writing, selling out‑of‑the‑money calls (0%–15% above the current price), as well as opportunistic credit call spreads, meaning in certain cases they sell calls and buy higher calls to capture part of the additional upside. All this with intensive use of FLEX options (customized options) and collateral in U.S. Treasury securities with maturities from 6 months to 2 years.

synthetic covered calls
classic standard covered call
opportunistic credit call spreads

Let me say it: a book of strategies that almost seems to tell the investor, “trust our management, we will manage to profit through various market phases.” That semi‑active management that often, however, is an illusion of security. But let’s get straight to the point… is it really so?

Partly Yes, But…

First of all, this “multi‑strategy option” approach has a cost: 4.76% when adding up the various items. A factor that certainly cannot be ignored.

And it has a low AUM of $147M, which shifts my concern to liquidity. A concern that is unfounded, because the bid/ask spread is 0.09%, therefore quite tight, and surprisingly, the average daily volume is over 930% higher than the average: so the slippage risk is reduced compared to other ETFs with that level of AUM.

But costs are secondary, and what we care about is performance: a variable element by definition, even though the yield since inception has been pleasantly steady, especially in distributions. As of now we are talking about a TTM yield of around 78%, paid monthly. A distribution that, judging from the 19a filings, is 97% ROC and only 2.59% income.

Considering that the SEC yield is 1.54%, it means the income naturally comes from option premiums. So, in my view, to really understand whether the multi‑strategy approach is or isn’t an “illusion of security,” I look at the holding distribution.

Today’s Composition Is Not The Same As Yesterday’s (Or Tomorrow’s)

But this is exactly its strength. The strategy changes, theoretically adapting to market conditions: in other words, it addresses one of the main criticisms, the statistical limits of buy‑write (covered call strategy). It’s therefore impossible not to mention the constant rolling of options and the high turnover (28% in the first half) along with high implicit costs. Today, its composition is this:

~50% options on IBIT ~45% Treasury Bills and Notes~1.7% Money Market FundCash & Other negative (–0.55%)

Peer Analysis: The Illusion Of Security

Quick thought: this dynamism gives YBIT a hedge. In the end, why not? Theoretically, it makes sense. The problem is that there is a chasm between theory and practice. Let me explain:

Let’s suppose an investor is interested in adding an ETF of this type to a portfolio, presumably to optimize cash flows and indirectly track, through total return, at least the performance of BTC, because they are a strong supporter of the decentralized market. Is YBIT the most suitable solution?

In this sense, it may help to know that, beyond YBIT, there are two other solutions worth considering: YBTC and BTCI.

Peer

  • YBTC: For the strategy, it uses ETFs on Bitcoin. It has a less dynamic structure, but in my opinion, it is similar to YBIT. With a medium‑aggressive distribution of about 42% annually (naturally variable), and a weekly distribution.

  • BTCI: it uses a Cayman subsidiary to hold Spot Bitcoin ETPs up to 25% of assets. It has a more conservative capital distribution compared to the first two, with a declared target of 25–30% annually in the prospectus, deliberately contained to preserve NAV (still actively managed, but there seems to be less flexibility in the strategies). It uses index options with Section 1256 treatment.

Risk And My Opinion

What to choose? A superficial answer: excluding YBTC, because it is very similar to YBIT, the most obvious answer is YBIT, given that it has a yield of more than three times BTCI’s target. Yet, in my view, over the long term it would be a less balanced solution.

My choice? With the same total return, I believe choosing the more conservative capital approach also optimizes cash flows over time (simply put, the yield leverages a larger principal). And since BTCI’s yield is still high (over 25%), I prefer BTCI.

But Beware

The multi‑strategy approach changes the picture; I told myself: the management might actually succeed in setting up a truly conservative multi‑strategy approach during periods of strong BTC swings, both negative and positive. This could lead YBIT’s performance to distance itself from BTCI’s.

Conclusion

But concretely, I prefer a solution that, regardless, manages better to preserve NAV, even though, clearly, this is not easy with BTCI either, especially over the long term and especially with option‑income‑oriented ETFs. In the end, these ETFs are like a scale: if you add too much weight to distributions, in my view these ETFs stop being core or long‑term satellite components of a portfolio and become tactical and strategic allocations for certain market phases. And YBIT, in my opinion, fully represents this setup, which is why, if I had to think of a yield‑generating alternative to Bitcoin to optimize income distribution, I would rather opt for solutions with more conservative NAV management and lower costs: in this sense, BTCI.

data.title: Fidelity Intermediate Municipal Income Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Municipal Market Review

U.S. policy uncertainty and unfavorable supply/demand dynamics crimped returns for investment-grade tax-exempt municipal bonds in the second quarter of 2025. The Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index returned -0.12% (including interest payments and price changes) for the three months. Against this backdrop of investor uncertainty, the index has returned -0.35% year to date.

Munis, like most U.S. bonds, struggled amid highly volatile global market conditions in April (-0.81%), triggered by the Trump administration's announcement of"reciprocal" tariffs on virtually all U.S. trade partners. Although munis typically are supported by domestic revenues and the credit of most state and local governments remained strong, they succumbed to trade-war worries. Yields on muni bonds, which move inversely to prices, were pulled higher by rising U.S. Treasury yields as investors feared that tariffs would reignite U.S. consumer inflation and forestall further policy interest-rate cuts by the Federal Reserve.

Unfavorable market technical conditions were another headwind.Heavy new-issue muni supply put more upward pressure on muni bond yields. After below-average issuance in 2022 and 2023- due to residual COVID-19 stimulus and a better-than-expected economic recovery from the pandemic-muni issuers returned to the market with record amounts of new debt deals.Additionally, some issuers pulled forward planned offerings, given uncertainty around the Trump fiscal and tax agenda.Feared changes to the municipal tax exemption weighed on munis as well, although the likelihood of that negative outcome mostly dissipated. By the end of April, munis had trimmed their losses relative to taxable bonds following President Trump's announcement of a 90-day pause on most tariffs. In May, municipals produced a roughly flat return (+0.06%).

Conditions remained volatile amid ongoing tariff uncertainty, as well as proposed sweeping tax cuts that were viewed as likely to add to the country's fiscal deficit. Resilient economic data and a "wait and see" stance from the Fed dimmed hopes for near-term interest-rate cuts, but muni demand significantly improved.

Munis then advanced 0.62% in June, a typically favorable month for the asset class. Sales of new municipal debt remained elevated, but investor demand continued to improve with the beginning of the annual seasonal pattern during which investors reinvest cash from muni bond maturities, coupons and redemptions.

For the full quarter, one-, three-, five-, seven- and 10-year municipal securities gained (+0.82%, +1.05%, +1.35%, 1.28%, and 0.78%, respectively), while 15-, 20-, and 22-plus-year securities lagged, returning-0.39%, -1.45% and-1.94%, respectively. From a sector perspective, revenue bonds (-0.20%) were roughly in line with general obligation bonds (0.00%). Among GOs, state-backed bonds (+0.60%) outpaced local government-backed debt (-0.43%).In the revenue category, the corporate-backed IDR/PCR sector (+0.55%) outpaced index segments that investors thought could be negatively impacted by the federal budget bill, including municipal hospitals (-0.95%) and higher education (-0.37%). Investment-grade municipals with higher ratings (AAA and AA) outpaced lower-quality debt rated A and BAA (the Bloomberg equivalent to BBB).

Muni tax-backed credit fundamentals remained mostly solid throughout the quarter. While the economic outlook has moderated and policy uncertainty has increased, the strong fiscal footing of most municipal issuers provides a degree of resilience against credit-rating downgrades.

THREE-MONTH MUNI INDEX RETURNS BY CREDIT QUALITY

A -0.24%
BAA -0.64%
Index -0.12%
Returns represent those of the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index.

Performance Review

DETAILED FUND ATTRIBUTION RELATIVE TO BENCHMARK

Strategy: Sector Allocation

Market Environment

General obligation bonds outperformed revenue bonds as yield spreads widened modestly, especially in the health care and higher education sectors.

● The fund's underweight in general obligation bonds detracted from performance versus the Bloomberg 2-17 Year Municipal Bond Index.(Negative)

Fund Positioning (Impact vs. Benchmark)

● Conversely, security selection in the housing and airport sectors contributed to relative performance.(Positive)

Strategy: Yield Curve

Market Environment

The muni yield curve steepened, as shorter-maturity yields fell and longer maturities rose.

Fund Positioning (Impact vs. Benchmark)

● The fund's yield-curve positioning detracted from relative performance.(Negative)

Strategy: Credit

Market Environment

Credit yield spreads widened modestly across rating categories.

● A higher average yield on the fund's underlying holdings helped it gain more carry than the index. (Positive)

•Underweights in bonds backed by the state of California and by New York City detracted.(Negative)

● Overweights in Los Angeles International Airport bonds,California tobacco bonds and Texas state bonds added value. (Positive)

Strategy: Additional Factors

Market Environment

The fund and its benchmark employ somewhat different methodologies in estimating the prices of municipal securities, most of which trade infrequently.

Fund Positioning

(Impact vs. Benchmark)

● Pricing methodology differences contributed.(Positive)

Outlook and Positioning

As of June 30, yields on longer-maturity municipal bonds stood near decade-high levels and were attractive relative to many comparable taxable bonds. This may present good risk/reward opportunities for investors, since starting yields are often the best predictors of future performance.

As for market technicals, we believe new-issue supply may revert to a historically normalized level and demand may remain firm, given munis' tax-exempt yields and the potential for increased reinvestment of cash from muni bond maturities, calls and redemptions, as is typical in the summer months.

It is important for investors to remember that Fidelity's municipal bond portfolios are constructed with a careful and intentional emphasis on issue selection, especially with consideration to the liquidity of the security and the financial resiliency of its issuer. In the current environment, we continue to evaluate each fund holding and are monitoring those that may be more financially challenged than others.

Going forward, and as always, we will seek to identify opportunities to purchase municipal securities that offer both attractive valuations and a solid risk/reward profile. At quarter end, we continue to invest for strong risk-adjusted returns while remaining cautious with yield-curve positioning, given the likelihood of ongoing interest-rate volatility.

We remain committed to the approach of building individual exposures in the portfolio that reflect risks with which we are comfortable, at entry prices we believe offer strong relative value.

FUND INFORMATION

Manager(s): Team Managed

Trading Symbol: FLTMX

Start Date: April 15, 1977

Size(in millions): $13,123.27

Morningstar Category: Fund Muni National Interm

Fixed income investments entail interest rate risk(as interest rates rise bond prices usually fall), the risk of issuer default, issuer credit risk and inflation risk. The municipal market can be affected by adverse tax, legislative or political changes and the financial condition of the issuers of municipal securities. Leverage can increase market exposure and magnify investment risk.Income exempt from federal income tax may be subject to state or local tax. All o a portion of the fund's income may be subject to the federal alternative minimum tax. Income or fund distributions attributable to capital gains are usually subject to both state and federal income taxes.

Investment Approach

● Fidelity Intermediate Municipal Income Fund is a diversified national municipal bond strategy investing primarily in intermediate-maturity general obligation and revenue-backed securities.

● Our investment approach focuses on fundamental credit analysis, yield-curve positioning and an analysis of the structural characteristics of each security.

● The fund's interest rate sensitivity is targeted closely to that of its benchmark to prevent interest rate speculation from overwhelming research-based strategies that we deem to have a higher likelihood of long-term success.

● We emphasize a total-return approach that seeks to generate a high level of tax-exempt income, consistent with the preservation of capital.

Definitions and Important Information

Unless otherwise expressly disclosed to you in writing, the information provided in this material is for educational purposes only. Any viewpoints expressed by Fidelity are not intended to be used as a primary basis for your investment decisions and are based on facts and circumstances at the point in time they are made and are not particular to you.Accordingly, nothing in this material constitutes impartial investment advice or advice in a fiduciary capacity, as defined or under the Employee Retirement Income Security Act of 1974 or the Internal Revenue Code of 1986, both as amended. Fidelity and its representatives may have a conflict of interest in the products or services mentioned in this material because they have a financial interest in the products or services and may receive compensation, directly or indirectly, in connection with the management, distribution, and/or servicing of these products or services, including Fidelity funds, certain third-party funds and products, and certain investment services. Before making any investment decisions, you should take into account all of the particular facts and circumstances of your or your client's individual situation and reach out to a professional adviser, if applicable.

CHARACTERISTICS

Duration is a measure of a security's price sensitivity to changes in interest rates. Duration differs from maturity in that it considers a security's interest payments in addition to the amount of time until the security reaches maturity, and also takes into account certain maturity shortening features(e.g., demand features, interest rate resets, and call options) when applicable. Securities with longer durations generally tend to be more sensitive to interest rate changes than securities with shorter durations. A fund with a longer average duration generally can be expected to be more sensitive to interest rate changes than a fund with a shorter average duration.

30-day SEC Yield is a standard yield calculation developed by the Securities and Exchange Commission for bond funds. The yield is calculated by dividing the net investment income per share earned during the 30-day period by the maximum offering price per share on the last day of the period. The yield figure reflects the dividends and interest earned during the 30-day period, after the deduction of the fund's expenses. It is sometimes referred to as"SEC 30-Day Yield" or"standardized yield".

30-Day SEC Restated Yield is the fund's 30-day yield without applicable waivers or reimbursements, stated as of month-end.

30-day SEC Tax-Equivalent Yield shows what you would have to earn on a taxable investment to equal the fund's tax-free yield, if you are in the 37% effective federal income tax bracket and also subject to the 3.8% Medicare Contribution tax, but does not reflect the payment of the federal alternative minimum tax, if applicable.Medicare Contribution tax is a tax on non-municipal investment income that applies to individuals with incomes over $200,000 (or$250,000, filing jointly). For state-specific funds, TEY is based not only on the highest federal tax rate(40.8%) but also the highest state tax rate. For state-specific funds, TEYs assume investors are state residents and would not be able to take an itemized deduction on their federal returns for state taxes on investment income. For NY funds, TEYs do not reflect the NY state tax rate that applies to income in excess of $5 million. For MD funds, TEYs reflect the highest city/county tax rates in MD and treat them the same as state taxes. Consult a tax professional for further detail.

Net Asset Value is the dollar value of one share of a fund;

determined by taking the total assets of a fund, subtracting the total liabilities, and dividing by the total number of shares outstanding.

Subject to Alternative Minimum Tax is the percent of interest income subject to the alternative minimum tax(AMT) based on total net assets within a portfolio.

IMPORTANT FUND INFORMATION

Relative positioning data presented in this commentary is based on the fund's primary benchmark(index) unless a secondary benchmark is provided to assess performance.

INDICES

It is not possible to invest directly in an index. All indices represented are unmanaged. All indices include reinvestment of dividends and interest income unless otherwise noted.

Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index is a market value-weighted index of investment-grade municipal bonds with maturities of one year or more.

Bloomberg 3-15 Year Blend(2-17) Municipal Bond Index covers the USD-denominated tax exempt bond market with a maturity between 2 and 17 years. It includes general obligation and revenue bonds, which both can be pre-refunded years later and get reclassified as such.

LIPPER INFORMATION

Lipper Averages are averages of the performance of all mutual funds with their respective investment classification category.The number of funds in each category periodically changes. Lipper,a Refinitiv company, is a nationally recognized organization that ranks the performance of mutual funds.

MORNINGSTAR INFORMATION

SECTOR WEIGHTS

Sector weights illustrate examples of market segments in which the fund may invest, and may not be representative of the fund's current or future investments. They should not be construed or used as a recommendation for any subset of the market.

Before investing in any mutual fund, please carefully consider the investment objectives, risks, charges, and expenses. For this and other information, call or write Fidelity for a free prospectus or, if available, a summary prospectus. Read it carefully before you invest.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

data.title: Fidelity Total Bond Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Taxable Bond Market Review

U.S.taxable investment-grade bonds gained 1.21% in the second quarter, as measured by the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, amid heightened volatility driven largely by market uncertainty around U.S. trade and fiscal policy. Year to date, the index has gained 4.02%.

The quarter got off to a dramatic start on April 2, when President Trump- proclaiming the day as Liberation Day-announced severe "reciprocal" tariffs on virtually all the U.S.'s trade partners, which he said were effective immediately. This led to a sharp but short-term selloff in the U.S. bond market, as prices fell and yields, which move inversely to prices, rose. Between April 4 and April 11, the yield on the benchmark 10-year U.S. Treasury rose from 4.01% to 4.48%, an extraordinary one-week spike. But the market stabilized shortly after the administration announced a 90-day pause on the tariffs (except those on China) on April 9. Notably, the yield on the 10-year Treasury closed the month exactly where it began, at 4.17%. The Aggregate index advanced a modest 0.39% for the month.

The index then returned -0.72% in May. During the month, investors became worried about the nation's fiscal trajectory as the administration's tax bill made its way through Congress. If passed in its current form, the budget legislation, dubbed the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, is expected to increase the national deficit by $2.4 trillion over the next ten years, according to the Congressional Budget Office. By late May, long-term (20- and 30-year) Treasury yields had risen above 5%, reflecting concerns about potentially persistent inflation and debt sustainability. On May 16, Moody's (MCO) became the last of the three large U.S. credit-rating agencies to downgrade U.S. Treasury debt, following similar actions by Standard & Poor's and Fitch in 2011 and 2023, respectively. All three now give U.S. debt their second-highest rating.

The market, however, rallied in June, rising 1.54%, helped by stable monthly inflation metrics and stronger-than-expected employment data. These developments contributed to a growing expectation that the U.S. Federal Reserve would resume its monetary easing program and implement policy interest-rate cuts in the second half of 2025, after pausing in the year's first half. Treasury yields declined across most of the curve during the month, with intermediate segments seeing the largest moves. For the full quarter, the yield curve steepened, as shorter rates fell slightly, the 10-Year Treasury was flat, and longer rates edged up.

Against this backdrop, intermediate bonds with maturities from three to seven years saw the strongest price performance, while investment-grade securities of 10 years or more declined slightly amid rising yields. By credit rating, bonds rated BAA (the Bloomberg equivalent to BBB) outperformed higher-quality issues.

Results at the sector level reflected an increased late-quarter appetite for risk, with yield-advantaged credit sectors outperforming Treasurys (+0.85%).U.S. corporate bonds (+1.82%) and commercial mortgage-backed securities (+1.88%) topped the investment-grade performance chart, bolstered by tightening credit spreads and resilient fundamentals, followed by government-related securities (+1.70%), asset-backed securities (+1.38%) and agency mortgage-backed securities (+1.14%).

Outside the Aggregate index, U.S. Treasury inflation protected securities lagged (+0.48%), per Bloomberg, while U.S. high-yield corporate bonds (+3.53%) and emerging-markets high-income securities (+3.75%) substantially outpaced their investment-grade counterparts, as spreads narrowed sharply later in the quarter as economic sentiment improved.

U.S. TREASURY YIELD CURVE

Source: Bloomberg

THREE-MONTH FIXED-INCOME SECTOR RETURNS

Sector Total Return Excess Return*
Government-Related 1.70% 0.65%
U.S. Mortgage-Backed Securities 1.14% 0.17%
Asset-Backed Securities 1.38% 0.27%
Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities 1.88% 0.39%
U.S. Corporate Investment Grade 1.82% 1.04%
U.S. Corporate High Yield 3.53% 2.17%
Emerging Markets: Investment Grade 1.81% 0.98%
Emerging Markets: High Yield 3.75% 2.60%
U.S. Treasury 0.85% 0.00%
Source: Bloomberg
*Over similar-duration Treasuries

Performance Review

DETAILED FUND ATTRIBUTION RELATIVE TO BENCHMARK

Strategy: Asset Allocation Strategy: Asset Allocation
Market Environment Non-investment-grade categories, including high-yield bonds and emerging-markets debt, outperformed investment-grade securities.
Fund Positioning (lmpact vs. Benchmark) The fund's out-of-benchmark exposure to certain"plus" categories, including high-yield securities, leveraged loans and non-investment-grade emerging- markets debt, contributed to performance versus the benchmark Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index. (Positive)
Strategy: Duration and Yield Curve Strategy: Duration and Yield Curve
Market Environment The U.S. Treasury yield curve steepened during the quarter, with rates falling at the front end and rising at the long end.
Fund Positioning (Impact vs. Benchmark) ● The fund's overall duration was slightly longer than that of the benchmark index, which helped relative performance.(Positive)

Strategy: Sector Allocation

Market Environment Most fixed-income assets advanced in Q2, with yield-advantaged sectors leading the way amid tighter credit spreads. Corporate bonds, commercial mortgage-backed securities and government-related securities notably outpaced U.S. Treasurys.
Fund Positioning (lmpact vs. Benchmark) ● The fund's positioning within investment-grade corporates, particularly an underweight in industrials, detracted from performance versus the Aggregate index.(Negative)
Strategy: Security Selection Strategy: Security Selection
Market Environment Among maturities, intermediate bonds fared best while long-term issues declined. By credit quality, securities rated BBB outperformed higher-quality investment-grade bonds.
Fund Positioning (Impact vs. Benchmark) ● Security selection was a neutral performance factor for the quarter. (Neutral)

Outlook and Positioning

At the midpoint of 2025, the global economic cycle remained in expansion, although it's become less synchronized across countries and more uncertain amid a variety of policy crosscurrents. The U.S. demonstrated a wide mix of dynamics, including solid mid-cycle indicators as well as signs of softening activity.

Outside the U.S., monetary easing provided a tailwind, as many central banks continued to cut interest rates, including the eurozone and U.K. The U.S. Federal Reserve held its policy rate steady, but at quarter end the market expected cuts totaling 0.5% to 0.75% (50 to 75 basis points) in the year's second half. Inflation remained above the Fed's 2% target throughout Q2.

Despite high volatility during the quarter, nominal 10-year U.S.Treasury yields finished about where they began, around 4.2%.Overall, yields remained modestly lower than where they started 2025, with real yields- the inflation-adjusted cost of borrowing- still at the high end of their range over the past decade. The U.S. dollar dropped to a three-year low in Q2, extending its year-to-date decline to more than 10%, per the ICE U.S. Dollar Index.

Versus the Aggregate index, the fund was overweight risk assets as of June 30 with a tilt toward intermediate investment-grade corporate credit, high-yield securities, leveraged loans and international credit. It remained underweight U.S. Treasurys, government agency mortgage-backed securities and long-dated investment-grade credit, and overweight asset-backed securities and commercial mortgage-backed securities. By credit rating, the fund was underweight bonds rated AA and A, and overweight securities rated BBB and lower. Overall, the portfolio was bulleted around the intermediate portion of the yield curve and, at period end, maintained a duration slightly longer than the index.

In a dynamic environment, we continue to find pockets of value within the fixed-income market, based on our view of pricing and fundamentals. Our goal remains to work with our investment teams to try to find attractively priced bonds for the portfolio while maintaining a disciplined approach to risk management.

It is important to reiterate that the portfolio is constructed with a careful emphasis on security selection, especially with consideration to liquidity and financial resiliency. Investing is a long-term endeavor, and we're focused on generating strong risk-adjusted performance over a full market cycle.

FUND INFORMATION

Manager(s): Celso Munoz, Ford O'Neil

Trading Symbol: FTBFX

Start Date: October 15, 2002

Size(in millions): $39,497.13

Morningstar Category: Fund Intermediate Core-Plus Bond

In general the bond market is volatile, and fixed income securities carry interest rate risk. As interest rates rise, bond prices usually fall, and vice versa. This effect is usually more pronounced for longer-term securities.) Fixed income securities also carry inflation risk, liquidity risk, call risk and credit and default risks for both issuers and counterparties.Unlike individual bonds, most bond funds do not have a maturity date, so avoiding losses caused by price volatility by holding them until maturity is not possible. Lower-quality bonds can be more volatile and have greater risk of default than higher-quality bonds.Foreign securities are subject to interest rate, currency exchange rate, economic, and political risks, all of which are magnified in emerging markets. Leverage can increase market exposure and magnify investment risk.

Investment Approach

● Fidelity Total Bond Fund is a diversified fixed-income strategy seeking competitive risk-adjusted performance commensurate with investor expectations of a core bond fund.

● The fund invests at least 80% of its assets in investment-grade bonds and up to 20% in non-investment-grade debt.

●Utilizing a team-based investment process, the fund relies on experienced portfolio managers, research analysts and traders. We concentrate on areas where we believe we can repeatedly add value, including asset allocation, sector and security selection, yield-curve positioning and opportunistic trading.

● Robust governance and risk management support the identification of both opportunities and risks.

CHARACTERISTICS

Duration is a measure of a security's price sensitivity to changes in interest rates. Duration differs from maturity in that it considers a security's interest payments in addition to the amount of time until the security reaches maturity, and also takes into account certain maturity shortening features(e.g., demand features, interest rate resets, and call options) when applicable. Securities with longer durations generally tend to be more sensitive to interest rate changes than securities with shorter durations. A fund with a longer average duration generally can be expected to be more sensitive to interest rate changes than a fund with a shorter average duration.

30-day SEC Yield is a standard yield calculation developed by the Securities and Exchange Commission for bond funds. The yield is calculated by dividing the net investment income per share earned during the 30-day period by the maximum offering price per share on the last day of the period. The yield figure reflects the dividends and interest earned during the 30-day period, after the deduction of the fund's expenses. It is sometimes referred to as"SEC 30-Day Yield" or"standardized yield".

30-Day SEC Restated Yield is the fund's 30-day yield without applicable waivers or reimbursements, stated as of month-end.

Net Asset Value is the dollar value of one share of a fund;determined by taking the total assets of a fund, subtracting the total liabilities, and dividing by the total number of shares outstanding.

YIELD CURVE

The relationship at a given point in time between yields on a group of fixed-income securities with varying maturities-commonly,Treasury bills,notes,and bonds.The curve typically slopes upward since longer maturities normally have higher yields, although it can be flat or even inverted.
